sentences.base_language: Ana is a student who is about to graduate and is looking for work.
sentences.target_language: Ana es estudiante y está a punto de graduarse, así que está buscando trabajo.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Está a punto de** + infinitive = is about to do something — a very common near-future construction: está a punto de llegar (is about to arrive), están a punto de empezar (they're about to start).
**Así que** = so/therefore (casual connector between clauses). **Está buscando** = is looking for — present progressive: **estar** + gerund (-ando/-iendo). Used for actions in progress right now, more emphatic than the simple present.

sentences.base_language: Today she has an interview for an engineer position and feels very nervous.
sentences.target_language: Hoy tiene una entrevista para un puesto de ingeniera y se siente muy nerviosa.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Un puesto de** = a position as — job titles after **de** drop the article: un puesto de gerente (a manager position), un trabajo de camarero (a waiter job).
Note gender: **ingeniera** (feminine) because Ana is female — Spanish nouns and adjectives agree in gender. **Se siente** = she feels — reflexive verb **sentirse**: me siento (I feel), te sientes (you feel), se siente (she/he feels). **Nerviosa** (feminine) agrees with Ana.

sentences.base_language: In the morning, she took a deep breath in front of the mirror and told herself: "No problem — I can do it."
sentences.target_language: Por la mañana, respiró hondo frente al espejo y se dijo: "No hay problema, puedo hacerlo."
sentences.grammarNote:
**Respiró hondo** = breathed deeply — **hondo** as an adverb (like respirar profundo). Common in fixed phrases: pensar bien (think carefully), hablar claro (speak plainly).
**Se dijo** = told herself (reflexive preterite of decir). **Puedo hacerlo** = I can do it — object pronouns attach to infinitives: **hacerlo** (do it), **decirlo** (say it). The same pronoun can also go before the conjugated verb: lo puedo hacer.

sentences.base_language: She had eggs and bread for breakfast, and drank a glass of milk.
sentences.target_language: Desayunó huevos y pan, y tomó un vaso de leche.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Desayunó** = she had breakfast (preterite of desayunar — a regular -ar verb). Spanish has a single verb for each meal: **desayunar** (have breakfast), **almorzar** (have lunch), **cenar** (have dinner).
**Tomó un vaso de** = had a glass of — **tomar** is used for eating and drinking in Spanish: tomar el desayuno, tomar una copa. **Un vaso de** = a glass of (for cold drinks); **una taza de** = a cup of (for hot drinks like coffee or tea).

sentences.base_language: The office was in the city centre, and she went by subway.
sentences.target_language: La oficina estaba en el centro de la ciudad y ella fue en metro.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Estaba** = was (imperfect of estar — for location in the past). **Fue** = went (preterite of ir, irregular). Note that **fue** is also the preterite of **ser** — context makes the meaning clear.
**En metro** = by subway — transport with **en**: en autobús (by bus), en avión (by plane), en coche (by car), en bicicleta (by bike). Exception: **a pie** (on foot). No article needed: fue en metro (not en el metro).

sentences.base_language: On the subway, her hands were sweating from nerves.
sentences.target_language: En el metro, le sudaban las manos de los nervios.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Le sudaban las manos** = her hands were sweating — an indirect object construction used for body-part sensations: literally 'to her, the hands were sweating.' This structure is essential in Spanish: le duele la cabeza (her head hurts), le temblaban las piernas (her legs were trembling), me duelen los pies (my feet hurt).
**De los nervios** = from nerves/anxiety — **de** expresses cause: llorar de alegría (cry from joy), temblar de frío (shiver from cold).

sentences.base_language: She arrived at the company building and glanced at her watch: nine-twenty.
sentences.target_language: Llegó al edificio de la empresa y miró su reloj: las nueve y veinte.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Llegó a** = arrived at (preterite of llegar + a for destination). **Miró** = looked at/glanced at (preterite of mirar). **Mirar** = to look at (intentional); compare **ver** = to see (perception).
**Las nueve y veinte** = 9:20 — telling time: **son las** + hour + **y** + minutes. Exceptions: **es la una** (1:00 — singular); **las nueve y media** (9:30); **las nueve menos cuarto** (8:45, lit. 'nine minus quarter').

sentences.base_language: The receptionist said: "How can I help you?"
sentences.target_language: La recepcionista le dijo: "¿En qué le puedo ayudar?"
sentences.grammarNote:
**¿En qué le puedo ayudar?** = How can I help you? (formal) — a polished customer service expression. **Le** here is the formal **usted** indirect object. Compare the informal: ¿En qué te puedo ayudar? or simply ¿Cómo te puedo ayudar?
**Usted** (formal you) uses 3rd person verb forms — le puedo ayudar, ¿cómo está usted? This is why Spanish has two 'you' registers that require different verb conjugations.

sentences.base_language: Ana replied: "Good morning. My name is Ana García and I have an interview at nine-thirty."
sentences.target_language: Ana respondió: "Buenos días. Me llamo Ana García y tengo una entrevista a las nueve y media."
sentences.grammarNote:
**Buenos días** = good morning — **días** is plural (a good-days greeting). The three: buenos días (morning), buenas tardes (afternoon/evening), buenas noches (night).
**Me llamo** = my name is, lit. 'I call myself' — reflexive of **llamarse**. Compare: **mi nombre es** (more formal). **A las nueve y media** = at nine-thirty — **a** for time: a las ocho (at eight), a las tres y cuarto (at a quarter past three).

sentences.base_language: The manager, Mr. Martínez, arrived, shook her hand, and said: "Nice to meet you."
sentences.target_language: El gerente, el señor Martínez, llegó, le dio la mano y dijo: "Mucho gusto."
sentences.grammarNote:
**Dar la mano** = to shake hands, lit. 'give the hand.' **Le dio la mano** = shook her hand — indirect object le + preterite of dar (irregular: di, diste, dio, dimos, dieron).
**El señor** = Mr. (used with last name in formal contexts: el señor Martínez, la señora García). **Mucho gusto** = nice to meet you — the standard first-meeting phrase, equivalent to **encantado/a** (pleased to meet you, with gender agreement).

sentences.base_language: Mr. Martínez asked her: "Why do you want to be an engineer?"
sentences.target_language: El señor Martínez le preguntó: "¿Por qué quieres ser ingeniera?"
sentences.grammarNote:
**¿Por qué...?** = why? — answered with **porque** (because, no accent, one word). A common learner error is writing *por que* or using the wrong form.
**Quieres** = you want (present of querer, stem-changing e→ie: quiero, quieres, quiere, queremos, quieren). **Ser ingeniera** — professions after **ser** take no article: soy médico, es abogada, quiero ser programador. The article would change the meaning: es un médico famoso (he's a famous doctor — specific).

sentences.base_language: Ana replied: "Because I love designing and building software to help people."
sentences.target_language: Ana respondió: "Porque me encanta diseñar y construir software para ayudar a la gente."
sentences.grammarNote:
**Me encanta** = I love it (lit. 'it enchants me') — **encantar** works like **gustar**: the thing loved is the subject, and the person is the indirect object. Me encanta (singular thing), me encantan (plural things): me encanta el café, me encantan las tapas.
**Para + infinitive** = in order to / to (purpose): para ayudar (in order to help), para aprender (to learn). Compare: **por** (reason/cause) vs **para** (purpose/goal).

sentences.base_language: Mr. Martínez nodded, smiled, and said: "Very good answers." He seemed very satisfied.
sentences.target_language: El señor Martínez asintió con la cabeza, sonrió y dijo: "Muy buenas respuestas." Parecía muy satisfecho.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Asintió con la cabeza** = nodded, lit. 'assented with the head' — body-part expressions use the definite article, not the possessive: con la cabeza (not *con su cabeza*). The same pattern: levantó la mano (raised his hand), cerró los ojos (closed her eyes).
**Parecía** = seemed (imperfect of parecer — an ongoing impression). **Satisfecho** = satisfied (past participle used as adjective, agrees in gender and number).

sentences.base_language: After the interview, Ana felt much calmer.
sentences.target_language: Después de la entrevista, Ana se sintió mucho más tranquila.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Después de** + noun/infinitive = after: después de la clase (after class), después de comer (after eating). **Se sintió** = felt (preterite of sentirse — stem-changing e→i: se sintió, se sintieron).
**Mucho más tranquila** = much calmer — **mucho** intensifies comparatives: mucho más grande (much bigger), mucho mejor (much better), mucho menos estresada (much less stressed). **Tranquila** (feminine) agrees with Ana.

sentences.base_language: She left the building and found a café nearby.
sentences.target_language: Salió del edificio y encontró un café cerca.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Salió de** = left/exited (preterite of salir + de for 'out of'). **Del** = de + el (required contraction before masculine nouns). The two contractions in Spanish: **al** (a + el) and **del** (de + el) — feminine nouns don't contract: salió de la oficina.
**Encontró** = found (preterite of encontrar, stem-changing o→ue in present, but regular in preterite). **Cerca** = nearby (adverb, used alone or as cerca de + noun: cerca del parque).

sentences.base_language: She ordered a coffee, sat down, and sent her mom a message.
sentences.target_language: Pidió un café, se sentó y le escribió un mensaje a su mamá.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Pidió** = ordered/asked for — preterite of **pedir** (stem-changing e→i in preterite: pidió, pidieron). **Pedir** = to order food/drinks or to ask for something. Compare: **preguntar** = to ask a question.
**Se sentó** = sat down (reflexive preterite of sentarse — stem-changing e→ie in present, but -ó in preterite). **Le escribió un mensaje a su mamá** — double marking of indirect object: **le** (pronoun) + **a su mamá** (noun phrase). Both are required in Spanish.

sentences.base_language: She wrote: "Mom, the interview is done! I think it went well for me."
sentences.target_language: Le escribió: "Mamá, ¡terminó la entrevista! Creo que me fue bien."
sentences.grammarNote:
**Creo que** = I think/believe that — **creer que** + indicative for factual beliefs. Contrast with subjunctive after verbs of doubt: no creo que sea fácil (I don't think it's easy).
**Me fue bien** = it went well for me — **irle bien a alguien** = to go well for someone. This is a key expression: ¿Cómo te fue? (How did it go for you?), me fue muy bien (it went very well for me), le fue mal en el examen (the exam went badly for him). Enormously useful for talking about outcomes.

sentences.base_language: Her mom replied quickly: "How wonderful! Tonight I'll make the fish you like most."
sentences.target_language: Su mamá respondió rápido: "¡Qué bueno! Esta noche preparo el pescado que más te gusta."
sentences.grammarNote:
**¡Qué + adjective!** = How + adjective! — an exclamation of reaction: ¡Qué bueno! (How great!), ¡Qué rico! (How delicious!), ¡Qué difícil! (How hard!). Very common and expressive.
**Preparo** = I'll make — the present tense used for an immediate future plan or commitment, especially in spoken Spanish: te llamo luego (I'll call you later), mañana voy (I'm going tomorrow). **Que más te gusta** = that you like most — relative clause: el que más me gusta (the one I like most).

sentences.base_language: Ana smiled, feeling wonderful. She sipped her coffee and looked at the street through the window.
sentences.target_language: Ana sonrió, con el ánimo muy bueno. Tomó su café y miró la calle por la ventana.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Con el ánimo muy bueno** = with great spirits — **ánimo** = spirits/mood/morale: levantar el ánimo (lift spirits), estar de buen ánimo (be in good spirits). Compare: **estado de ánimo** (emotional state/mood).
**Por la ventana** = through the window — **por** for movement through or perspective: mirar por la ventana, entrar por la puerta (enter through the door). Compare **para**: this expresses route/means, not purpose.

sentences.base_language: Whatever the result, she knew she had already given her best.
sentences.target_language: Fuera cual fuera el resultado, sabía que había dado lo mejor de sí misma.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Fuera cual fuera** = whatever it might be — imperfect subjunctive for concessive/hypothetical: **fuera cual fuera** (whatever it was), **pasara lo que pasara** (whatever might happen), **dijera lo que dijera** (whatever she might say). These are elegant fixed patterns worth memorising.
**Había dado** = had given — pluperfect tense (había + past participle): for actions completed before another past moment. **Lo mejor de sí misma** = the best of herself — **sí misma** is the reflexive pronoun for 3rd person: de sí mismo (of himself), de sí misma (of herself).
